name: Sri Lanka
tagline: From the hydraulic kingdoms of Anuradhapura to an island republic.
summary: Sri Lanka's recorded history reaches back more than two millennia, to Sinhalese kingdoms that built one of the world's great hydraulic civilisations around the city of Anuradhapura and embraced Buddhism in the 3rd century BCE. After medieval capitals at Polonnaruwa and, later, Kandy, the island's coasts passed in turn to Portuguese, Dutch and British control, with the whole island unified under Britain in 1815 and remade as a plantation economy. The Dominion of Ceylon won independence on 4 February 1948 and became the Republic of Sri Lanka in 1972. The modern nation has been shaped by a long civil war (1983–2009), the catastrophic 2004 tsunami, and a severe economic crisis in 2022.

founding.label: Independence from Britain
founding.detail: The Dominion of Ceylon became independent on 4 February 1948, when its 1947 constitution came into force, ending British colonial rule that had governed the unified island since 1815. D. S. Senanayake became the first prime minister. In 1972 a new constitution declared the country a republic and changed its name from Ceylon to Sri Lanka, though its statehood traces to Sinhalese kingdoms more than two thousand years older.

quickFacts.label: Civilisational roots
quickFacts.value: Anuradhapura kingdom, from c. 4th century BCE

quickFacts.label: Buddhism introduced
quickFacts.value: 3rd century BCE, by the missionary Mahinda

quickFacts.label: Republic
quickFacts.value: 1972 constitution; renamed Ceylon to Sri Lanka

quickFacts.label: Civil war
quickFacts.value: 1983–2009, ending with the defeat of the LTTE

eras.title: Anuradhapura and the Coming of Buddhism
eras.standfirst: Sinhalese kingdoms centred on Anuradhapura built a great hydraulic civilisation and made the island a heartland of Theravada Buddhism.
body: Sinhalese tradition, recorded in the chronicle known as the Mahavamsa, traces the island's settlement to Prince Vijaya and his followers in the 5th century BCE, and attributes the founding of the kingdom of Anuradhapura to Pandukabhaya, third king of the Vijaya line. Modern archaeology complicates this legend: settled agriculture, tank irrigation, iron use and pottery were present before the Indo-Aryan migrations, and early Sinhalese culture emerged from a synthesis of Indo-Aryan, pre-Indo-Aryan and Dravidian elements during the Anuradhapura period, which spanned roughly the 3rd century BCE to the 10th century CE.

Around the 3rd century BCE a mission sent during the reign of the Mauryan emperor Ashoka brought Buddhism to the island. Its leader, Mahinda, met the Sinhalese king Devanampiya Tissa near the Mihintale hill outside Anuradhapura and converted him, making the king a powerful patron of the new faith. The Mahavihara monastery at Anuradhapura became a historic centre of Theravada Buddhism, and a sapling of the sacred Bodhi tree, brought by Mahinda's sister Sanghamitta, was planted there. Buddhism became central to Sinhalese identity and statecraft.

Anuradhapura flourished for some thirteen centuries as a religious and political capital. Its kings raised colossal brick stupas and, above all, mastered water: an intricate network of tanks (reservoirs) and channels turned the dry northern plain into productive rice land, a feat that earned the era the name of a hydraulic civilisation. From the 2nd century BCE the kingdom was repeatedly invaded from South India, and after a Chola conquest at the end of the 10th century the capital was finally abandoned in favour of Polonnaruwa around 1017.

events.title: Sinhalese settlement and the founding of Anuradhapura
events.summary: By tradition Prince Vijaya's followers settle the island; the kingdom of Anuradhapura is founded under Pandukabhaya and grows into the strongest Sinhalese state, capital for over a thousand years.

events.title: Buddhism introduced under Devanampiya Tissa
events.summary: The missionary Mahinda, sent during the reign of the Mauryan emperor Ashoka, converts King Devanampiya Tissa near Mihintale, establishing Theravada Buddhism as the island's defining faith.

events.title: Hydraulic civilisation and South Indian invasions
events.summary: Anuradhapura's kings build vast reservoir-and-channel systems to irrigate the dry plain, while recurrent invasions from South India culminate in the Chola conquest and the abandonment of the capital around 1017.

figures.name: Devanampiya Tissa
figures.role: King of Anuradhapura, patron of Buddhism
figures.blurb: The Sinhalese ruler converted by Mahinda's mission, he became the first great royal patron of Buddhism in Sri Lanka, founding the Mahavihara monastery and receiving the sacred Bodhi tree sapling at Anuradhapura.

pullquote.text: Anuradhapura flourished for 1,300 years as a political and religious capital before it was abandoned after invasion.

eras.title: Polonnaruwa and the Move South
eras.standfirst: A second classical capital at Polonnaruwa peaked under Parakramabahu I before drought, invasion and political decline pushed the Sinhalese kingdoms toward the south-west and the hills.
body: After Anuradhapura fell, Polonnaruwa rose as the island's second capital, reaching its zenith in the 12th century under Parakramabahu I (reigned 1153–1186). A builder-king in the old hydraulic tradition, he is famed for the vast reservoir at Polonnaruwa known as the Parakrama Samudra, the 'Sea of Parakrama', and for a vision that no drop of rain should reach the sea unused. The garden-city he created, with its palaces, monasteries and monumental rock-cut Buddha images, survives as one of Sri Lanka's foremost archaeological sites.

Polonnaruwa's prosperity did not last. Renewed invasions from South India, the silting and decay of the great irrigation works, and the spread of malaria in the dry zone undermined the northern heartland. From the 13th century the centre of Sinhalese power drifted south-west and into the central highlands, fragmenting into competing kingdoms. The dry-zone civilisation that had sustained millions for over a thousand years gradually emptied, its tanks and cities reclaimed by jungle.

By the later medieval period several kingdoms shared the island. In the hill country the kingdom of Kandy emerged as the principal independent Sinhalese state, its mountainous setting making it remarkably difficult for outside powers to subdue. A Tamil kingdom centred on Jaffna held the north. This patchwork of states, with no single ruler over the whole island, was the political landscape that European powers encountered when they reached Sri Lanka's shores in the 16th century.

events.title: Polonnaruwa becomes the capital
events.summary: Following the Chola conquest and the abandonment of Anuradhapura, Polonnaruwa is established as Sri Lanka's second great capital in the north-central dry zone.

events.title: Golden age under Parakramabahu I
events.summary: Parakramabahu I rebuilds the city and its irrigation, creating the immense Parakrama Samudra reservoir and a garden-capital that marks the high point of the Polonnaruwa period.

events.title: Decline and the drift southward
events.summary: Invasion, the breakdown of the dry-zone irrigation system and disease lead to the abandonment of the northern capitals and the migration of Sinhalese power toward the south-west and the Kandyan hills.

figures.name: Parakramabahu I
figures.blurb: The greatest of Polonnaruwa's kings, he unified the island under his rule, restored its irrigation works and built the colossal Parakrama Samudra reservoir, embodying the ideal that not a drop of water should be wasted.

eras.title: European Coasts and the Kandyan Kingdom
eras.standfirst: Portuguese and then Dutch powers seized the island's lucrative coasts and cinnamon trade, while the Kandyan kingdom held the interior for three centuries.
body: In 1505 a Portuguese fleet under Lourenço de Almeida was driven into Colombo, beginning more than a century of Portuguese presence on the coasts. Drawn by Ceylon's cinnamon and other spices, the Portuguese built forts at Colombo, Galle and elsewhere and extended control over much of the littoral and the northern Tamil kingdom of Jaffna, while pressing Roman Catholic missions. They could never, however, master the highland kingdom of Kandy, whose terrain and resistance kept it independent.

Seeking to break the Portuguese monopoly, the Dutch East India Company allied with Kandy and, in a series of campaigns, captured Galle in 1640 and finally expelled the Portuguese with the fall of Colombo in 1656 and Jaffna in 1658. The Dutch ruled the maritime provinces for nearly 140 years, prizing above all the island's cinnamon, which they regarded as the world's finest. Their legacy survives in the great fortifications of Galle, an exceptional example of a European fortified town adapted to South Asian conditions.

Throughout these two colonial centuries the kingdom of Kandy preserved the last independent Sinhalese state and remained the custodian of Buddhism and the Tooth Relic. It resisted European powers for over two hundred years until 1815, when, amid conflict between the king and his own chiefs, the Kandyan aristocracy invited British intervention. By the Kandyan Convention, signed at Kandy on 2 March 1815, the kingdom was annexed to Britain's other holdings, bringing the entire island under a single ruler for the first time.

events.title: Arrival of the Portuguese
events.summary: A Portuguese fleet reaches Colombo, opening more than a century of Portuguese control over Ceylon's coasts and its profitable cinnamon trade.

events.title: Dutch supplant the Portuguese
events.summary: Allied with Kandy, the Dutch East India Company takes Galle (1640) and expels the Portuguese with the fall of Colombo (1656) and Jaffna (1658), ruling the maritime provinces for cinnamon.

events.title: Kandyan Convention unifies the island under Britain
events.summary: The Kandyan chiefs cede the highland kingdom to the British crown, ending the last independent Sinhalese kingdom and placing the whole island under a single colonial administration.

pullquote.text: Founded in the 16th century, Galle is the best example of a fortified city built by Europeans in South and South-East Asia.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO, Old Town of Galle and its Fortifications

eras.title: British Ceylon and the Road to Independence
eras.standfirst: Britain remade the unified island as a plantation colony of coffee and tea, bringing Indian Tamil labour to the highlands, before Ceylon negotiated a peaceful path to independence in 1948.
body: Under British rule the whole island, now governed as the single colony of Ceylon, was transformed into a plantation economy. From the 1830s European capital opened coffee estates in the central highlands; when coffee leaf rust destroyed that crop in the late 19th century, tea took its place and became the colony's economic backbone, alongside rubber and coconut. To work the estates the British brought large numbers of indentured Tamil labourers from southern India between roughly the 1830s and 1930s, creating a distinct Indian (or Hill-country) Tamil community that came to form the bulk of the plantation workforce.

Colonial rule also brought roads, railways, English-language schooling and a Westernised elite, but it dispossessed the Kandyan peasantry and entrenched divisions among the island's communities. In the 20th century a constitutional reform movement gathered pace. Ceylon gained universal adult suffrage in 1931, unusually early for a colony, and a generation of moderate, largely English-educated leaders pressed for self-government through negotiation rather than mass struggle.

After the Second World War Britain conceded full independence. A new constitution took effect and, on 4 February 1948, the Dominion of Ceylon became independent within the Commonwealth, with D. S. Senanayake as its first prime minister and the British monarch still represented by a governor-general. The transition was comparatively smooth, but the constitutional settlement left unresolved tensions over language, religion and the status of the Tamil minorities that would dominate the decades to come.

events.title: Plantation economy and Tamil labour migration
events.summary: British coffee and later tea estates transform the highlands; indentured South Indian Tamil labourers are brought in to work them, forming the Indian Tamil community of the plantation districts.

events.title: Universal suffrage and reform
events.summary: Constitutional reforms grant universal adult suffrage in Ceylon, far ahead of most colonies, strengthening a moderate movement for self-government.

events.title: Independence of the Dominion of Ceylon
events.summary: Ceylon becomes an independent dominion within the Commonwealth as its 1947 constitution takes effect, with D. S. Senanayake as first prime minister.

figures.name: D. S. Senanayake
figures.blurb: Often called the father of the nation, he led Ceylon to independence through negotiation and served as its first prime minister from 1947 until his death, championing irrigation and land-settlement schemes that resettled hundreds of thousands.

eras.title: Republic, Civil War and the Modern Nation
eras.standfirst: Independent Ceylon became the Republic of Sri Lanka, endured a long civil war and the 2004 tsunami, and confronted a severe economic crisis in 2022.
body: Post-independence politics were quickly reshaped by Sinhalese nationalism. In 1956 S. W. R. D. Bandaranaike's Sri Lanka Freedom Party swept to power and passed the Official Language (Sinhala Only) Act making Sinhalese the sole official language, with support for Buddhism and Sinhalese culture; the measure deepened grievances among the Tamil minority. Bandaranaike was assassinated by a Buddhist monk in 1959. In 1972 a new constitution declared the country a republic and changed its name from Ceylon to Sri Lanka, and a further constitution in 1978 created a powerful executive presidency.

Tensions over language, education and land erupted into open conflict. After anti-Tamil riots in July 1983, remembered as Black July, the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE) waged an armed campaign for an independent Tamil state in the north and east. The civil war lasted, with intervals, until the government forces decisively defeated the LTTE in May 2009, ending some twenty-six years of fighting that killed tens of thousands. The conflict and especially its final phase remain the subject of disputed accounts and international concern over the conduct of both sides.

Two great shocks bracket the modern era. On 26 December 2004 the Indian Ocean tsunami struck the coasts, killing more than 30,000 people in Sri Lanka and displacing hundreds of thousands, the island's worst natural disaster. In 2022 a balance-of-payments crisis pushed the country into its first-ever sovereign default; shortages of fuel, food and medicine triggered mass protests that drove President Gotabaya Rajapaksa from office. An IMF programme and debt restructuring followed, and in 2024 voters elected an outsider, Anura Kumara Dissanayake, on an anti-corruption platform as the nation worked toward economic recovery.

events.title: Ceylon becomes the Republic of Sri Lanka
events.summary: A new republican constitution ends dominion status and changes the country's name from Ceylon to Sri Lanka, while it remains within the Commonwealth.

events.title: Civil war between the state and the LTTE
events.summary: After the anti-Tamil violence of Black July in 1983, the LTTE fights for a separate Tamil state; the war ends with the group's military defeat in May 2009 after about twenty-six years.

events.title: Indian Ocean tsunami
events.summary: Waves from a magnitude-9.1 undersea earthquake off Sumatra devastate Sri Lanka's coasts, killing more than 30,000 people in the country's deadliest natural disaster.

events.title: Economic crisis and sovereign default
events.summary: A foreign-exchange crisis forces Sri Lanka's first sovereign default; shortages and protests topple President Gotabaya Rajapaksa, leading to an IMF bailout and, in 2024, the election of Anura Kumara Dissanayake.

figures.name: S. W. R. D. Bandaranaike
figures.blurb: Founder of the Sri Lanka Freedom Party, he came to power in 1956 on a wave of Sinhalese nationalism, making Sinhalese the sole official language and elevating Buddhism in public life before his assassination by a Buddhist monk in 1959.

pullquote.text: In 1972 a new constitution made the country a republic and changed its name from Ceylon to Sri Lanka.

figures.name: Mahinda
figures.blurb: Sent on a mission during the reign of the Mauryan emperor Ashoka and described as his son, Mahinda converted King Devanampiya Tissa near Mihintale and established Theravada Buddhism on the island, a turning point in Sinhalese civilisation.

figures.name: Parakramabahu I
figures.blurb: The pre-eminent ruler of the Polonnaruwa period, he unified the island and built the colossal Parakrama Samudra reservoir, the high point of Sri Lanka's ancient hydraulic civilisation.

figures.name: D. S. Senanayake
figures.blurb: The father of Sri Lankan independence, he negotiated the island's peaceful transition to self-rule and became its first prime minister in 1947, promoting irrigation and resettlement on the model of the ancient tank civilisation.
